one_line_takeaway: Sequentially patching LLMs with independently-evaluated safety/privacy/fairness defenses causes measurable, highly order-dependent risk regressions in 38.9% of 144 tested compositions (including two cases worse than the undefended base model), traceable to anti-aligned parameter updates and activation-trajectory reversals in a small set of shared critical layers, which a lightweight layer-freezing mitigation can substantially offset.
key_finding: 38.9% of the 144 ordered sequential defense compositions (56/144) exhibit measurable risk exacerbation (RRR>0) on the dimension the primary defense had targeted, including two Catastrophic Collapses (RRR>1) where the final model ends up more vulnerable than the undefended base model: DPO->RMU on Llama-S (RRR=1.02) and Unbias->RMU on Qwen-S (RRR=1.11). Conflicts are highly asymmetric and order-dependent: reversing DPO->RMU to RMU->DPO on the same model flips the outcome from catastrophic collapse to synergy (RRR<0); likewise Unbias->CAT causes severe conflict on all 6 models (RRR in [0.40,0.70]) while CAT->Unbias is mostly neutral/synergistic. Fairness-first deployment is the most fragile regime (64.6% conflict rate, 31/48), more than double Safety-first (25.0%, 12/48) and above Privacy-first (27.1%, 13/48). Privacy defenses applied first show unusual resilience to subsequent interventions, contrasting with prior reports that unlearning is fragile under generic downstream fine-tuning [ref 49]. Smaller models show higher aggregate conflict rates (43.1% vs 34.7% for large models), though the relationship is non-monotonic (reverses in the Fairness-first regime: 70.8% large vs 58.3% small). Mechanistically, conflicting sequences show strongly anti-aligned parameter updates in shared critical layers (PCS=-0.6865 for Seq I: DPO->RMU) versus near-orthogonal updates in benign orderings (PCS=-0.0925 for Seq II: RMU->DPO), and PCS correlates with RRR across all 144 compositions (Spearman rho=-0.511, p<0.001). PCA trajectory analysis shows conflicting sequences exhibit activation trajectory reversals in shared layers (e.g., Layer 7 in Seq I) while benign sequences show directionally consistent, accumulating shifts. The layer-wise Conflict Score CS is positive (synergistic) in layers 3-5 (+0.25, +0.18, +0.13) but turns negative in layers 6-7 (-0.03, -0.10) for the DPO/RMU pair, matching where conflict is empirically observed. Conflict-guided layer freezing recovers protection in all 3 tested cases: on Llama-S, freezing Layer 7 during RMU training averts the safety regression entirely (safety risk 0.663->0.510, i.e., improves versus DPO-only 0.530) while preserving privacy gains (0.035 vs 0.037 unfrozen); on Qwen-S it reduces the Unbias->RMU catastrophic collapse from a fairness score of 29.88 (worse than base 26.98) down to 5.38, close to the post-Unbias value of 1.12.
core_contribution: The paper presents the first systematic empirical and mechanistic study of how sequentially deployed LLM defenses (targeting different risk dimensions: safety, privacy, fairness) interact and interfere with one another. The authors build CONFLICTEVAL, a framework that formalizes pairwise sequential defense composition (M2 = D2(D1(M0))), defines a Relative Regression Rate (RRR) metric to quantify how much of D1's protection is eroded by a subsequently applied D2, and evaluates 144 ordered defense compositions spanning 3 risk dimensions x 6 defense methods x 3 model families x 2 scales. They then conduct a mechanistic analysis (layer-wise representational divergence, activation patching, PCA trajectory analysis, a Parameter Conflict Score, and a layer-wise Conflict Score) to explain why some sequential defense orderings collapse while others coexist, and propose 'conflict-guided layer freezing' as a lightweight mitigation.
threat_model: Defender: an LLM service provider (e.g., Meta/DeepSeek/OpenAI-like) that incrementally applies post-training defenses (safety alignment, privacy unlearning, fairness mitigation) to an already-deployed model over its lifecycle, without full retraining. Adversary: a black-box end user with query-only access (no parameters, gradients, training data, or defense-pipeline visibility) who is aware that providers apply sequential post-training patches and probes for regressions in risk dimensions that were previously mitigated (e.g., re-eliciting unsafe generations that an earlier safety defense had suppressed). Security objective: a newly introduced defense must not invalidate protections established by an earlier defense; a 'successful attack' is a sequential composition that produces measurable regression on a previously defended risk dimension.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) study is restricted to pairwise defense compositions -- three-or-more-defense chains may show qualitatively different dynamics; (2) only 6 representative defenses across 3 risk dimensions are evaluated -- other paradigms (RLHF-based alignment, differential-privacy training) may show different interference patterns; (3) conflict-guided layer freezing is a proof-of-concept that does not jointly optimize all defense objectives -- a principled co-optimization framework is left to future work. Additionally noted in-text: the mechanistic activation-trajectory/CS analysis is only performed on 3 representative sequences (not all 144) because per-sequence layer-wise localization and activation tracing is computationally prohibitive; population-scale validation instead relies on the coarser PCS proxy, whose correlation with RRR (rho=-0.511) is only moderate, and the paper itself flags that 'direct causal verification at scale remains an open challenge.' Several causal claims about why particular algorithm pairs behave as they do (e.g., TV vs. Unbias stability, DPO's localized updates) are explicitly hedged by the authors as plausible interpretations rather than confirmed mechanisms.

datasets_used: TOFU (privacy fine-tuning base + forget sets for RMU/NPO and Extraction Strength evaluation)
StereoSet (Unbias and Task Vector fairness training; StereoSet Score fairness evaluation)
SALAD-Bench (404 filtered Malicious Use / Representation & Toxicity prompts for Attack Success Rate)
quantitative_results: 38.9% (56/144) of ordered compositions show RRR>0 (measurable risk exacerbation)
2 Catastrophic Collapses (RRR>1): DPO->RMU on Llama-S RRR=1.02; Unbias->RMU on Qwen-S RRR=1.11
Small-model overall conflict rate 43.1% vs large-model 34.7%; Fairness-first reverses this (58.3% small vs 70.8% large)
Max MMLU accuracy drop across all 144 sequences: under 3 percentage points
Parameter Conflict Score: Seq I (conflicting, DPO->RMU) = -0.6865; Seq II (benign, RMU->DPO) = -0.0925
Spearman correlation between PCS and RRR across all 144 compositions: rho=-0.511, p<0.001
Layer-wise Conflict Score (DPO vs RMU, Llama-S): +0.25 (Layer 3), +0.18 (Layer 4), +0.13 (Layer 5), -0.03 (Layer 6), -0.10 (Layer 7)
Conflict region layer sets: Seq I {6,7}; Seq II {5,6}; Seq III {9,15}
Mitigation Case I (Llama-S, Base->DPO->RMU): safety risk Base=0.660, +DPO=0.530, +DPO+RMU=0.663, +DPO+RMU frozen L7=0.510; privacy risk 0.707->0.713->0.037->0.035
Mitigation Case II (Gemma-S, Base->Unbias->CAT): fairness risk Base=14.88, +Unbias=2.98, +Unbias+CAT=8.08, +frozen L9=6.56; safety risk 0.559->0.661->0.319->0.298
Mitigation Case III (Qwen-S, Base->Unbias->RMU): fairness risk Base=26.98, +Unbias=1.12, +Unbias+RMU=29.88 (catastrophic, exceeds base), +frozen L6=5.38; privacy risk 0.190->0.131->0.067->0.030
Unbias->CAT (Fairness-first) RRR range across 6 models: [0.35, 0.70] (severe, consistent conflict)
CAT following NPO (Privacy-first) RRR range: [0.02, 0.07] across 5/6 models
baselines_compared: Undefended base model M0 (used as the reference point in RRR denominator and for detecting catastrophic collapse)
Single-defense (D1-only) intermediate model M1, used as the pre-secondary-defense reference for measuring Delta and RRR
Unconstrained sequential deployment (+D1+D2, no freezing) as the baseline the layer-freezing mitigation is compared against
Six defense methods compared pairwise against each other within/across risk dimensions: DPO, CAT (safety); RMU, NPO (privacy); Unbias, Task Vector/TV (fairness)
